Participants completed a multitasking simulation with four tasks distributed across physical and virtual displays under four display configurations: two configurations with spatial discontinuity, one cross-display configuration without discontinuity, and a baseline configuration where all tasks were shown on the physical display. Each configuration was tested under two task load levels. Results showed that OST-HMD-supported AR significantly impaired multitasking performance, regardless of whether spatial discontinuity between virtual and physical displays was present. Introducing display discontinuities through small depth separations or alignment offsets produced no additional significant influence on performance. The AR configurations also shown increased perceived workload than the baseline, but spatial discontinuity did not significantly affect workload ratings. Task load modulated the effect of display configurations on performance as well as physical and frustration ratings of the perceived workload.

title: Two-timescale Adaptive Dynamic Event-triggered Control of Multi-agent Systems via a Singular Perturbation Approach
authors: Kun Liang, Jiayin Hou, Huaicheng Yan, Hao Shen, Chuanlin Zhang, Mengshen Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: In numerous practical systems, the dynamics inherently involve multiple time scales. For example, in motors, power electronic, and other electrical circuits, fast electromagnetic transients coexist with slow mechanical motions. Motivated by this observation, this paper develops a two-timescale event-triggered control strategy tailored for such multi-timescale systems. To conserve communication resources, an efficient two-timescale dynamic event-triggered mechanism is designed, in which an auxiliary variable is introduced to dynamically adjust the triggering threshold, while a nonlinear function is introduced to further accelerate the convergence of the error system, balancing the system performance and bandwidth resource. On this basis, a two-timescale adaptive controller is proposed to adjust the network coupling strength, which depends on the slow-fast timescale parameters. Furthermore, by leveraging the characteristics of network topology, the consensus conditions are extended to a situation where computational complexity is independent of the number of network nodes, in which case the adaptive parameter is constant. Finally, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, two illustrative examples including an electric circuit network and five single-link manipulators are provided.

title: Deep Fuzzy C-means Clustering in a Federated Heterogeneous Scenario
authors: Longmei Li, ZhenSheng Zang, Nan Zhu, LiYong Zhang, Wei Lu, Witold Pedrycz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Federated Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering methods have demonstrated success in standard federated learning (FL) environments, but model heterogeneity presents a significant challenge. This heterogeneity hampers communication content selection and makes traditional aggregation methods, such as federated averaging (FedAvg), insufficient for diverse client models. Additionally, handling non-IID data distributions and learning robust feature representations remain unresolved in some existing approaches. To tackle these issues, we propose FFCMD, a federated FCM clustering framework designed to address model heterogeneity while enhancing feature learning and accommodating non-IID data. FFCMD integrates an autoencoder (AE) with a fuzzy C-Means network (FCMN), augmented by mutual knowledge distillation (mKD), to improve data feature learning, communication, and aggregation under heterogeneous conditions. The AE incorporates a clustering-oriented penalty term to learn discriminative features, boosting performance on complex datasets. Rather than direct parameter aggregation, mKD updates the global model using local devices' soft assignments, naturally handling structural variations across client models. Moreover, FFCMD employs discrepancy-aware weighting mechanisms to dynamically align local and global category distributions during soft assignment embedding, effectively addressing the challenges posed by non-IID data. Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets show that FFCMD outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly in non-IID scenarios, and excels even in the presence of model heterogeneity.

title: Can AI Models Truly Replace Scientific Computing? Examining Claims, Assumptions, and Evidence
authors: Elias Dritsas, Maria Trigka
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) models are increasingly being discussed, both explicitly and implicitly, as potential replacements for traditional scientific computing methods, raising fundamental questions regarding their suitability for scientific computation. This article critically examines these claims by assessing whether contemporary AI models satisfy the practical requirements of scientific computation. The analysis shows that while AI models offer substantial value as computational accelerators and surrogate components, they lack the systematic error control, generalization guarantees, and physical accountability required for reliable replacement. This gap explains why replacement narratives remain controversial and motivates a more precise and responsibility-aware role for AI in scientific and engineering workflows.

title: Fair and Risk-Averse Worker Selection in Mobile Crowdsourcing via Mean-Variance Bandits
authors: Ziqun Chen, Kechao Cai, Jinbei Zhang, John C.S. Lui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Mobile crowdsourcing is recognized as a promising paradigm for harnessing collective wisdom to solve problems. The crowdsourcing platform assigns tasks to a group of workers (e.g., vehicles, sensors) and receives the outcomes from them. In this paper, we develop a novel combinatorial mean-variance bandit model for worker selection and characterize the quality of workers with mean-variance measures to consider both the reward and risk in task assignment. Moreover, we introduce a utility-based fairness constraint to ensure a fair selection of workers. We consider two types of feedback: quantitative feedback, where the platform can receive numerical rewards from the selected workers, and preference feedback, where the platform only receives a qualitative performance ranking of the selected workers. We define the mean-variance regret and fairness regret and propose novel bandit algorithms that balance the fairness guarantee and mean-variance optimization in worker selection under both feedback settings. We prove that our algorithms achieve sublinear expected mean-variance regret and fairness regret. Through extensive simulations, we validate that our algorithms can fairly select workers while maximizing the mean-variance of selected workers.

title: Machine Learning-Assisted Dynamic Multi-Objective Optimization for Enhancing SMT Quality and Efficiency
authors: Junhu Cao, Jinyong Yu, Zhengkai Li, Hao Sun, Xinghu Yu, Tong Wang, Huijun Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Quality and efficiency objectives in surface mount technology (SMT) production are inherently conflicting, posing a significant challenge for collaborative optimization. Dynamic factors, such as process parameter anomalies and mechanical failures, substantially increase the complexity of SMT production optimization. However, existing research typically addresses efficiency or quality objectives separately. This article integrates automatic optical inspection (AOI) feedback into the surface mounter, introducing closed-loop quality optimization on top of the original efficiency optimization to enhance overall production performance dynamically. A multi-objective mixed integer programming model is first formulated to enable dynamic head workload assignment for small-scale problems. For large-scale instances, offline optimization generates Pareto-optimal solutions that balance SMT quality and efficiency for practical production planning by utilizing an improved MOEA/D algorithm with decision tree-based operator selection. This algorithm integrates component clustering encoding, heuristic decoding, and adaptive operator selection. Online dynamic optimization is subsequently performed in response to AOI inspection feedback. A dynamic head workload adjustment heuristic is utilized to maximize efficiency while ensuring that quality metrics are met. These two parts constitute the machine learning-assisted dynamic multi-objective optimization (ML-DMO) framework. Experimental validation demonstrates that ML-DMO achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art algorithms in both multi-objective optimization convergence and practical production efficiency, while enabling dynamic workload adjustment in response to real-time quality feedback.

title: A Lightweight PCB Defect Detection Algorithm Based on an Improved YOLOv11s
authors: Xuhui Ye, Lilu Huang, Jun Wang, Xinyu Hu, Jing Xu, Chupeng Zhang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: To address the limited accuracy of existing PCB surface defect detection methods, the challenge of balancing detection performance with lightweight design, and the need for real-time deployment in electronic manufacturing systems, we propose a lightweight PCB defect detection algorithm based on an improved YOLOv11s. First, a lightweight EfficientViT module is introduced into the backbone to replace the original backbone, reducing computational resource usage while enhancing inter-channel information exchange. Second, we integrate FasterNet partial convolution (PConv) with an efficient multi-scale attention mechanism (EMA), and design a C3k2_Faster_EMA convolution to replace standard convolutions in the neck module, further decreasing parameter count and strengthening fine-grained feature extraction. Finally, by combining Inner_IoU auxiliary bounding boxes with the dynamic focusing mechanism of Wise_IoU, we construct an Inner_Wise_IoU loss to replace the original CIoU loss, improving localization accuracy for small targets and bounding-box regression performance. Experiments show that the improved method achieves strong performance on the PKU-Market-PCB and DeepPCB datasets, attaining an mAP of 92.3%. Compared with the original YOLOv11s, parameters are reduced by 61.7%, computational complexity by 63.9%, and model size by 58.3%, down to 8.0MB, while reaching an inference speed of 227.54 FPS. To validate deployment feasibility on edge devices, we deploy the model on the LubanCat-5 platform. The results indicate that the quantized model size is 6.1MB, with an average frame rate of 23.094/s and an average inference time of 43.301ms; relative to the non-quantized version, the frame rate increases by 130% and inference time decreases by 56%. These results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a favorable balance between lightweight design and detection accuracy, offering strong engineering value and practicality for modern electronic manufacturing systems.

title: Theory and Application of Pulse-and-Glide for Electric Vehicles with Various Powertrain Topologies
authors: Kai Man So, Davide Tavernini, Umberto Montanaro, Patrick Gruber, Danylo Trinchuk, Matic Herzog, Aldo Sorniotti
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Most of the available pulse-and-glide (PnG) studies focus on internal combustion engine driven and hybrid-electric vehicles. PnG has been recently extended to electric vehicles (EVs), but without detailed consideration of the powertrain losses during the glide phase. Moreover, control allocation (CA) implementations for EVs focus on the optimal torque allocation among multiple motors to reduce energy consumption, while meeting vehicle dynamics constraints. However, PnG has not been used in conjunction with CA so far, since the available optimal CA formulations target the minimization of instantaneous power consumption for an assigned total wheel torque demand, and are thus incompatible with PnG. This paper reformulates the CA problem such that PnG becomes a viable optimization solution. The formulation also includes powertrain power losses during glide. The result is a linear programming (LP) optimization problem, which can be efficiently solved. An alternative convex hulls graphical method is also discussed, generating identical solutions to the LP. From the graphical approach, the conclusion is that, for PnG to be beneficial, the battery power consumption (i.e., DC link side of the inverter) vs. torque curve must present non-convexities. When an EV has multiple identical motors, an infinite number of energy-optimal solutions exist. Rules are developed to select the best among the possible solutions, with heuristic consideration of drivability and passenger comfort. Simulation results, evaluating multiple PnG options for a sport utility EV with onboard and in-wheel powertrains, show up to 20% and 8% energy consumption reductions, respectively, at speeds below 50 km/h, compared to non-PnG operation.

title: A Multimodal and Fine-Tuned Large-Language Model for Health Assessment of Underground Power Cables
authors: Rudai Yan, Changyou Suo, Yan Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: Health assessment of underground cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) power cables is crucial for maintaining reliable operation of a power grid. Existing methods are mostly based on the condition data, without sufficient utilization of the water tree formed in the cables, a very important health indicator. To makefull use of heterogenous data of cable health information, this letter proposes a multimodal large-language model (LLM) method for cable health assessment, which simultaneously uses water tree images and condition monitoring data, including insulation resistance (IR), IR Ratio, very low frequency tan δ, and age, as the input and generates a numerical health index. For training data collection, lab experiments are conducted to capture water tree images of retired cables. Then, the images and cable condition data are combined to fine-tune a LLM through low-rank adaptation (LoRA), which is highly computational efficient and requires much less memory. The proposed method is implemented with Gemma 3-4B and tested on real-world underground cable data.

title: Genetic Algorithm-Enhanced CMILK for Robust Thermal Facial Data Imputation
authors: Yean Chun Ng, Alexander G. Belyaev, F.C.M. Choong, Shahrel Azmin Suandi, Joon Huang Chuah, Bhuvendhraa Rudrusamy
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Missing data are ubiquitous in real-world datasets and can substantially degrade the performance of statistical analyses and deep learning models when essential features are absent. Imputation methods address this problem by replacing missing entries with plausible values that preserve the underlying data structure. In this work, we evaluate CMILK (Correlation-based Multiple Imputation with Local k-neighbour matching) for imputing missing thermal facial landmark temperatures and present a Genetic Algorithm (GA)–based procedure to optimise its hyperparameters. Comparative studies verified the effectiveness of GA, while ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of the GA-driven optimisation and its impact on convergence behaviour. GA-optimised CMILK achieves consistent improvements over the baseline: mean absolute error (MAE) decreases from 0.5724 to 0.5479 (a 4.3% relative reduction) and root mean square error (RMSE) from 0.7657 to 0.7371 (a 3.7% relative reduction). Hypothesis testing confirms that the observed reduction in RMSE after GA optimisation is statistically significant, supported by consistent parametric and nonparametric analyses with a large effect size and positive confidence interval. The optimisation also widens CMILK’s lead over the next-best method to 5.0% in MAE and 7.0% in RMSE, while producing a modest runtime improvement of 12.1% versus the baseline. These results show that automated hyperparameter search using GA produces measurable gains in accuracy and efficiency, improving CMILK’s reliability for predictive modelling and robust analysis of thermal facial-landmark temperature data.

title: DEM: Dendritic Learning-Incorporated Enhanced 3D Mamba for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Shuaibing Shi, Min Li, Yujie He, Wenjing Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Currently, most research on hyperspectral image classification focuses on optimizing feature extraction networks to obtain more representative and discriminative spectral-spatial features. However, how to effectively classify the extracted features has rarely been explored. Meanwhile, the issue of losing fine-grained local details remains inadequately resolved. To address these challenges, we propose a framework that integrates dendritic learning and enhanced 3D Mamba (DEM). We design a dendritic network classification head (DNCH) that mimics the structure of biological neurons, enabling the network to integrate features more effectively. Additionally, we introduce a simple and flexible cross-feature spectral-spatial attention module (CFSSAM) to highlight target-related fine-grained local features. Experimental results on two benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed DEM surpasses state-of-the-art methods in HSI classification. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/shike801/DEM.

title: Source Code Repo-Based Candidate Comparison System for Recruitment of Software Engineers
authors: Gaurang Agrawal, Tirth Bhadani, Ankush Tyagi, Harshadkumar B Prajapati, Vipul K. Dabhi
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Identifying competent applicants for software development roles represents a crucial responsibility that influences organizational achievement. This research introduces an innovative source code repository-driven candidate evaluation system that assesses and prioritizes applicants using metrics including repositories, pull requests, commits, programming language proficiency, repository recognition, and contribution regularity patterns. In contrast to current methods that depend on manual review or complicated multi-dimensional assessments, our framework delivers immediate, clear, and language-focused evaluations. The suggested method helps recruiting managers by significantly cutting down on evaluation time, offering objective indicators, and facilitating data-driven hiring choices while preserving process transparency. We evaluate our approach compared with existing methods and demonstrate improved results in efficiency, transparency, and practical implementation. Furthermore, our framework incorporates language-focused filtering features and contribution regularity examination through creative heatmap displays, enabling hiring managers to assess applicants according to proficiency in particular programming languages and work regularity patterns.

title: ACO-QL Based Drone Route Optimization for Medical Supply Delivery During Emergencies
authors: Richa Singh, Prajeet Sharma, Jayram Dwivedi, Sarita Sahni, Anand Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Data, Energy and Communication Networks (DECoN)
abstract: Efficient and reliable delivery of medical supplies is critical in emergencies, especially in areas with disrupted or unavailable infrastructure. This study proposes a drone route optimization framework based on hybrid ant colony optimization algorithm with machine learning (ACO-QL) to address this challenge. This method leverages the global search capability of ant colony optimization and reward-based adaptive learning in machine learning to determine optimal flight paths for autonomous drones under realistic conditions. The system models geographic data from Bhopal, India, incorporating factors such as distances between locations, drone power consumption, communication delays, and restricted nofly zones (such as airport areas). While the ACO system simulates the collective intelligence of ant agents to explore routes using pheromone-based decision-making, the Q-learning system continuously updates route decisions based on dynamic environmental feedback, improving adaptability and performance over time. The framework is validated through iterative simulations using optimized paths, animated performance visualization. The results show that the ACO-QL approach improves route efficiency, security, and reliability compared to static or single-modal solutions. This work demonstrates the potential of intelligent group learning and reinforcement-based navigation for immediate, autonomous, drone-based medical logistics in high-risk emergency situations.

title: Design and Validation of EMI/EMC-Compliant Analog Sensor PCB
authors: Arth Jaiswal, Nehal Kandele, Archana Sharma, Nitesh K. Bharadwaj
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: In this paper we have developed EMI/EMC compliant PCB of analog temperature sensor with voltage regulator. Here we have presented the design cycle of the circuit from the first stages of idea to hardware validation. We used a thermistor based temperature sensor in our design also a discrete PMOS voltage regulator. We had a very in depth circuit design process which was paired with a multi tool validation approach which included the use of the open source layout tool KiCad and the commercial EM simulation software CST Studio Suite in addition to that we also did physical laboratory tests. Throughout the design, we concentrated on signal integrity (SI) and power integrity (PI). EMI/EMC performance assessed against CISPR standards. The findings indicate complete adherence with the industrial EMC regulations, providing a comprehensive system to develop resilient PCB in flexible open source tools.

title: Cybersecurity in Digital Finance: Artificial Intelligence-Powered Fraud Detection and Risk Management
authors: Nagireddy Karri, Sandeep Kumar Jangam, Partha Sarathi Reddy Pedda Muntala
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Cybersecurity in digital finance has become a critical area due to the growing number of online transactions and the increasing sophistication of financial fraud. Digital platforms, mobile wallets, and real-time payment systems are widely adopted, but they often face threats such as identity theft, unauthorized access, transaction manipulation, and synthetic fraud. Traditional rule-based fraud detection methods fall short in identifying complex and adaptive attack patterns, especially in high-speed environments. This paper introduces an artificial intelligence (AI)powered framework for fraud detection and risk management tailored to digital finance systems. The proposed model brings together supervised learning, anomaly detection, and behavioral analytics. A deep learning model processes transaction sequences, user metadata, and device information to capture both shortterm anomalies and long-term behavioral shifts. The approach supports real-time scoring and risk classification by integrating autoencoder-based anomaly filters and ensemble classifiers. A risk-aware decision module also adjusts fraud detection thresholds based on transaction value and context. To evaluate the proposed framework, a real-world financial transaction dataset with labeled fraud instances was used. The model achieved high performance across multiple metrics, including an F1-score of 0.94, an AUC of 0.97, and a precision of 92.8 %. Compared to baseline models such as logistic regression, decision trees, and standard neural networks, the proposed framework showed significant improvements in early fraud detection and low false positive rates. These results highlight its ability to reduce financial loss, maintain transaction security, and support trust in digital payment systems. The approach is scalable to large datasets and adaptable to new fraud trends, making it suitable for modern financial infrastructures.

title: Support Vector Regression and the Detection of Geopolitical Risk Anomalies in Emerging Markets
authors: Tran Trong Huynh, Bui Thanh Khoa
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: This research examines the role of geopolitical risk (GPR) in shaping stock returns in Vietnam's frontier market by embedding macroeconomic uncertainty into a machine learning-based asset pricing framework. The analysis uses monthly observations from the Hanoi Stock Exchange (HNX) spanning 2005-2024 and employs Support Vector Regression (SVR) within a 36-month rolling window to forecast next-month excess returns, conditional on market excess return and movements in the global GPR index constructed by Caldara and Iacoviello. Stocks are then sorted by predicted returns into ten decile portfolios to build a long-short strategy (P11). The strategy delivers an average monthly excess return of 1.31%, significant at the 5% level. Subsequent CAPM and Fama-French threefactor regressions report positive and statistically significant alpha coefficients, demonstrating that these abnormal returns cannot be explained by standard risk factors. Robustness checks across alternative rolling windows, kernel functions, and subperiods yield consistent results. The findings highlight a novel anomaly driven by the interaction between GPR and SVR forecasts, demonstrating that machine learning methods can effectively capture nonlinear risk-return dynamics in emerging markets. This research contributes to the literature by (i) incorporating geopolitical risk into an ML-based pricing framework, (ii) validating SVR's predictive power, and (iii) revealing a new source of excess returns in Vietnam.

title: Human Authentication Using Gait Feature Extraction System
authors: Mayank Shekhar, Amit Kumar Trivedi, Ripon Patgiri, Jasvinder Pal Singh
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: The real analysis of gait biometric patterns has high potential for biometric-based person recognition and identification and in healthcare. Leveraging computer vision concept, data processing and machine learning, this from this extracts and examines neighboring features from gait videos for accurate identification. The proposed system processes gait information to improve identification accuracy and reliability in real-time, improving security and reducing false positives. Its modular structure permits versatility across a series of applications, e.g., medical with security access control. The solution counteracts the limitation of standard biometric methods in that it delivers an invasion-free identification solution, making novel possibilities available for security and tailored health-care applications.

title: Optimization of LMA-10 and LMA-25 PCF for Low-Loss Optical Transmission in High-Power Applications
authors: Sonu Kumar, Rajesh Kumar, Ghufran Mahmood, Rahul Kumar, Km Priyanka, Ritu Raj Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Electronics Engineering (ICE3)
abstract: The optimization of Large Mode Area (LMA) Photonic Crystal Fibers (PCFs) is critical for enhancing optical transmission efficiency and minimizing losses in high-power applications. This manuscript investigates the impact of key geometric parameters - pitch (M), air hole radius (r), and the number of cladding rings - on confinement loss, mode quality, and overall transmission performance. The results indicate that a pitch of $15 \mu m$ provides an optimal balance between mode confinement and fabrication feasibility, significantly reducing confinement losses for LMA-25. An air hole radius of $3 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ enhances the refractive index contrast and confinement efficiency, leading to improved optical performance without introducing structural instabilities for LMA-10. Additionally, 4 to 6 cladding rings effectively suppress higher-order modes and improve beam quality. Among the designs studied, LMA-10 exhibited the lowest confinement loss ($1.2272 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~dB} / \mathrm{km}$) and the largest mode effective area ($47.1679 \mu^{2}$), making it highly suitable for high-power optical applications.

title: A Frequency-Aware Suppression Masking for Generalized Deepfake Detection
authors: Sneha Ilame, Ahlad Kumar
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Deepfake detection continues to pose a significant challenge due to the growing capabilities of generative models. Most current approaches often overfit to specific manipulation artifacts and struggle to perform reliably across diverse generative techniques. Following recent advances in frequency masking, inspired by masked image modeling, we propose a Frequency-Aware Suppression Masking strategy to improve generalization across various generative sources. Our approach employs frequencyaware masking by applying band-specific suppression during training and encouraging the model to learn generalized feature representation instead of manipulation-specific artifacts. We tested our proposed method using FFT and DCT transforms and observed that DCT-based masking yields stronger generalization. We evaluated across a range of generative models in different augmentation settings and demonstrated that our method improves generalization and also outperforms baseline approaches, confirming its effectiveness for robust deepfake detection.

title: Analysis of spectral characteristics of Kaiser Window Function for various application
authors: Yatresh Mishra, Harsh Vikram Singh, Anurag Singh, Eti Srivastava, Shweta Singh, Pallavi Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Electronics Engineering (ICE3)
abstract: Window method is one of most convenient and popular method for the designing of FIR filter. We know the Kaiser Window function has adjustable behaviour as well as superior performance as compared to other window function currently in use which make it more convenient and suitable for the designing of FIR filter. In this paper we will analyse the spectral characteristics parameter like Main-lobe width, leakage factor, Side lobe roll-off ratio and ripple ratio of Kaiser Window function for different value of controlling parameter beta and window size. The research work’s goal is to obtain the most appropriate window size and controlling parameter on which the Kaiser Window function has better performance. Based on outcomes we can say that the Kaiser Window function has superior performance for the various applications like bio-medical image processing, Speech signal filtering, geophysical image processing and signal filtering.

title: A Planar Inverted F Antenna for LoRa Network with Enhanced Bandwidth for IoT Application
authors: Kumar Vaibhav Srivastava, Rajan Mishra, Rajeev Kumar Chauhan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Electronics Engineering (ICE3)
abstract: This research focuses on a Planar Inverted Antenna (PIFA) for Long Range (LoRa) applications. The proposed design is a meander slot PIFA to cover the lower frequency of 868 MHz, which is preferable for the LoRa module used on the Internet of Things (IoT) applications. The dimensions of the meander slot antenna are $40 \mathrm{~mm} \times 110 \mathrm{~mm}$. The designed antenna is also used in fire alarms, industrial sites for continuous observation, and hospitals. The transmission using the LoRa module is half-duplex communication, so it cannot be suitable for real-time applications, and due to the slow data rate, it is unusual for voice transmission. various IoT applications such as smart homes, smart cities, IoT-enabled offices, and small area network systems. The operating frequency of this antenna is 868 MHz, so it is suitable for transmission through the LoRa network module. This antenna provides a return loss of −16.17 dB, peak gain of 1.8 dB, radiation efficiency is 60% and a bandwidth of 54 MHz.

title: Modeling the Operating Modes of a Propulsion Electric Motor as Part of a Shipboard Power System
authors: Nikita Yu. Filimonov, Dmitrii S. Gulnev, Ilyas R. Laypanov, Polina V. Timoshkova, Egor A. Krivoborsky
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Power, Energy &amp; Industry Applications (ElCon-PEIA)
abstract: The research aims to develop mathematical models and control algorithms for the PEM, optimizing its operation under extreme conditions while minimizing negative impacts on the shipboard network.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Security and Governance (ICAISG)

title: Machine Learning for Android Malware Detection: A Closer Look at Model Performance and Generalization
authors: Vijetha U, Spurthy Maria Pais, Shruti Patil
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Machine learning algorithms have emerged as a popular choice of tool for Android malware detection. Many research efforts have been reported in literature with exceptional malware detection performance on well-known datasets. But, malware problems are still on the rise, forcing us to question the reliability of the models for malware detection. We experiment with ML models on standard datasets, as well as merged and de-biased datasets. Our analysis reveal that good performance of models on selected datasets may not necessarily mean effective malware detection, rather it may indicate that the models may be exploiting dataset specific traits and learning shortcuts to prediction. We performed feature activation analysis on various datasets to reveal biases and specific traits that may be misleading the models to make certain predictions. Our work highlights the need for a careful consideration of dataset bias and distribution traits when developing malware detection models for real-world applicability

title: Single-Exposure High Dynamic Range Image Sensor using Thin-Film Photodiode Pixel
authors: Minhyun Jin, Myonglae Chu, Pawel E. Malinowski, Itai Lieberman, Jan Genoe, Jiwon Lee
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: This paper presents a single-exposure high dynamic range (HDR) image sensor based on a thin-film pinned photodiode (TF-PPD) pixel structure. The proposed technique efficiently exploits the unique structural features of the TF-PPD architecture to achieve high dynamic range operation while retaining the inherent advantages of thin-film photodiodes, such as wavelength extensibility and ease of integration. Specifically, the photodiode (PD) under the photogate (PG) is utilized as a large photodiode (LPD), while the PD in the floating diffusion (FD) region is employed as a small photodiode (SPD). This enables single-exposure HDR imaging without requiring additional circuitry or complex fabrication steps. Furthermore, by adjusting the integration-time ratio between the LPD and SPD, an additional effective dynamic range can be achieved. The prototype image sensor demonstrates an effective dynamic range of 100 dB with an integration-time ratio of 5:1 between the LPD and SPD. The proposed technique successfully reproduces both bright and dark regions under single-exposure conditions, confirming its capability for efficient HDR imaging within a compact pixel design.

title: Extending Aumann-Shapley for Reliable Neuron Attribution in Autonomous Driving Decisions
authors: Rui Shi, Tianxing Li, Yasushi Yamaguchi, Liguo Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: The rapid evolution of autonomous driving necessitates a clear understanding of the decision-making processes within these complex systems. While traditional path integral methods, which identify critical inputs by integrating gradients, offer a potential solution for attributing model predictions, their direct application to autonomous driving models often yields unreliable and counterintuitive results. This paper identifies two primary reasons for these shortcomings: the use of unreliable attribution baselines and integration paths. To address these problems, we propose a reliable method that constructs an open connected space for the baseline, specifically adapted to the unique attributes of driving scenes. Additionally, we implement baseline constraints to ensure the baseline accurately represents features absent in decision-making, thereby creating a solid foundation for accurate attribution computation. We also adjust the integration paths to accommodate the dispersed nature of objects in driving scenarios by using gradient-weighted feature maps, which helps in reducing noise and improving the reliability of attribution results. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of our method, providing not only reliable and interpretable attributions but also outperforming state-of-the-art explanation techniques in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

title: 2025 International Conference On Emerging Computation and Information Technologies (ICECIT)
authors: N/A
publish: N/A

title: DeFiMix: Indistinguishable Coin Mixing Schemes in Decentralized Finance
authors: Yuan Chang, Yinbin Miao, Tom H. Luan, Jinkai Zheng, Zhou Su
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The need for enhanced transaction privacy in decentralized finance (DeFi) is critical. However, existing coin mixing solutions often reveal telltale patterns on the blockchain, exposing users to heuristic analysis. This paper presents DeFiMix, an indistinguishable coin mixing scheme engineered to obscure transaction flows while guaranteeing fairness and security. DeFiMix achieves this through a dual-layer mechanism. First, an off-chain secret handshake protocol enables anonymous negotiation between senders and mixers, effectively breaking the link between transactions and participants. Second, on-chain transactions are structured using time-locks and concurrent signatures to resemble common DeFi activities such as staking and lending, rendering them indistinguishable from ordinary operations. Using security analysis and extensive simulations, we validate DeFiMix’s ability to prevent transaction linkage while remaining practically viable. The results underscore DeFiMix’s strong indistinguishability and fairness, alongside its minimal computational demands, establishing it as a compelling solution for privacy-focused transactions within the DeFi ecosystem.

title: IEEE Professional Communication Society Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Professional Communication

title: Data-Driven Tuned Fast Extremum-Seeking Control with Sliding Mode Adaptation Law for Microgrid Frequency Support
authors: Ahsan Iqbal, Niranjan Bhujel, Astha Rai, Ujjwol Tamrakar, Timothy M. Hansen, Reinaldo Tonkoski
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Frequency deviations and large rate of change of frequency (ROCOF) are significant concerns in low-inertia microgrids due to the dominance of converter-based generation. These disturbances lead microgrids towards instability if unaddressed. Previously, model predictive control (MPC) strategies have been employed in energy storage systems (ESS) to provide fast-frequency support (FFS). However, MPC relies on accurate dynamic models and limits adaptability. Fast extremum-seeking control (FESC) offers a model-light alternative. Existing FESC schemes are underdeveloped for dynamic applications like microgrid FFS, where performance measures evolve with system state dynamics. Therefore, traditional FESC exhibits limitations, including suboptimal performance due to variable response of system states at perturbation frequency; moreover, it offers slow convergence and reduced robustness due to integral and proportional-integral (PI) adaptation laws. In this paper, a data-driven FESC (DD-FESC) framework is proposed and employed for microgrid FFS. The DD-FESC utilizes active probing system identification (APSI) to estimate average system dynamics for designing a state compensator that dynamically adjusts state response at DD-FESC perturbation frequency to enable effective optimization; moreover, the identified model initializes tuning of controller parameters. After initialization stage, the controller operates without requiring the system model. A sliding mode control (SMC) based adaptation law with Lyapunov-like stability is proposed to ensure rapid convergence and enhanced robustness of controller. Comparative simulation studies in Matlab/Simulink on a modified microgrid benchmark system from Cordova, Alaska, demonstrate that the proposed DD-FESC outperforms established FESC schemes and MPC in terms of performance, convergence speed, and robustness, enabling dynamic FFS in microgrids.

title: A Magnetizing Current Controlled Dual-Input Flyback based Microinverter
authors: Aaqib Ahmad Qureshi, Subhendu Dutta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: This paper proposes a dual input flyback based microinverter (MI) in which two input photovoltaic (PV) panels are operated at their respective maximum power points under varying environmental conditions, e.g. insolation difference on the two panels. The power circuit of the proposed MI comprises of a high frequency DC-DC flyback conversion stage where the input and output of individual flyback stage are connected in series. Each flyback stage can independently regulate its associated PV input to enable maximum power extraction while generating a rectified sinusoidal voltage and current waveform at the output stage of it. This voltage and current are unfolded with a grid frequency unfolding stage, which interfaces with the single phase AC grid. A magnetizing current control scheme is implemented by using a single current sensor for each DC-DC stage. This control approach in CCM eliminates the right half plane zero in the current to duty transfer function of a flyback stage, resulting in increased bandwidth and improved dynamic response. No sensors are used to sense the output voltages of the DC-DC stages, rather they are estimated, thereby reducing the voltage sensing requirement of individual DC-DC stage. The topological configuration of the proposed MI achieves a high DC voltage gain with reduced transformer turns ratio and duty cycle as compared to a single-input single-output (SISO) MI. As compared to a SISO MI, the power harvesting becomes twice while the voltage gain requirement reduces to half. The leakage current issue of the proposed MI is mitigated by the inherent high frequency galvanic isolation stage of the power circuit. The working of the proposed MI along with its control strategy have been validated through a detailed simulation and experimental studies on a 200 W prototype. However, only experimental results are provided to adhere to the page limit of the initial submission.

title: This Lecture Makes Me Sick: on Confounding Factors Influencing the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
authors: Tim Weissker
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) has become a standard tool for quantifying the severity and distribution of discomfort symptoms in virtual reality (VR) research. Despite its straightforward administration, the use of the SSQ also comes with significant challenges, including response subjectivity, strict threshold values based on a military reference population, and confounding factors influencing the results. To demonstrate the adverse interplay of these issues, we asked three cohorts of students to fill in a SSQafter having attended a 90-minute lecture of our teaching program. Although students were not exposed to any form of VR experience, the resulting SSQ scores were indistinguishable from VR studies and extended far beyond the originally defined threshold of a “bad simulator”, with 88.1% of TS scores being larger and 25.4% even exceeding thrice this value. We compare our results to alternative scoring systems of the SSQ proposed in the literature and suggest implications for future experimental designs involving the quantification of sickness symptoms. In summary, our results motivate to exert caution when interpreting the results of the SSQ in the context of a VR study; participants might just have attended a lecture prior to the experiment.

title: A Synergetic Framework for Efficient and Sustainable Internet of Medical Things
authors: S. Jagadeesh Babu, Subhasri Duttagupta, Roshan Ayyalasomayajula
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Reliable operation of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) is critical for patient safety and operational efficiency of healthcare systems. The objectives of resource management of battery-operated devices in IoMT are to extend device longevity and minimize environmental impact. Current IoMT frameworks often treat resource efficiency and carbon sustainability as independent objectives, without a mechanism to resolve conflicts that arise when energy conservation and emission reduction goals compete simultaneously during clinical emergencies. Most of these frameworks employ strategies that compromise the precision of drug delivery or environmental responsibility during emergencies. This study introduces synergy, an ondevice unified IoMT framework that integrates a Resource Efficiency Agent and a Sustainability Agent under a Centralized Decision Policy (CDP) engine, which arbitrates inter-agent conflicts by prioritizing safety, clinical precision, energy conservation, and carbon sustainability in sequence. It incorporates techniques such as adaptive sampling, hierarchical caching, Markov Decision Process (MDP) policy-driven resource allocation, and real-time carbon emission tracking. Synergy was validated across 157 infusion cycles using a clinical-grade micro-infusion pump as a representative single-device laboratory evaluation. The results demonstrated net energy savings of 28.3%, a 15 to 20% reduction in carbon footprint, and a 26% increase in the operational lifespan of the device, while maintaining drug delivery accuracy within 1% of the clinical safety threshold. The results indicate that the framework can improve healthcare efficiency and simultaneously meet environmental sustainability goals.

title: Tide: Intra-and Inter-Timeslot Enhanced Node Embedding for Probing Attack Detection in SDN
authors: Longyan Ran, Yunhe Cui, Guowei Shen, Chun Guo, Yi Chen, Qing Qian
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Probing the configurations of the Software-Defined Networking (SDN) switches is the essential preliminary for attacking SDN. This study points out a critical bottleneck in detecting probing attack: the dynamically changing character of different kinds of probing attacks makes the existing detection methods fail to detect probing attack. In this paper, we propose Tide, a probing attack detection method based on a Dynamic Flow-Packet graph (DFP-Graph), along with an intra-and inter-timeslot enhanced node embedding strategy. Tide constructs a (FP-Graph) across multiple timeslots, thereby modeling the intra-timeslot DFP-Graph consisting of multiple static Flow-Packet Graphs correlations that include the flow to flow, packet to packet, flow to packet, and packet to flow relationships while representing the inter-timeslot correlation of flows. Furthermore, Tide proposes an intra-timeslot node embedding module, an inter-timeslot node embedding module, and a probing attack flow detection module. The intra-timeslot node embedding module is designed to update the node representations based on the intra-timeslot correlation among different flows, while the inter-timeslot node embedding module is proposed to track the time-varying characters of a single flow. Finally, the probing attack flow detection module is employed to integrate the flow nodes’ representations in each timeslot and identify the probing attack flows. The experimental results demonstrate that Tide can effectively detect probing attack. It achieves the average detection accuracy at 87.51%, which outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

title: Exploring Microbridge Size Effects on the Electrical Properties of Superconducting Titanium Bolometers
authors: Qian-Jing He, Jia-Qiang Zhong, Wei Miao, Pei-Zhan Li, Zheng Wang, Qiang Zhi, Ming Yao, Jiang-Qiao Ding, Kang-Min Zhou, Yuan Ren, Wen Zhang, Jing Li, Sheng-Cai Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: This study explores the influence of microbridge dimensions on the electrical properties of superconducting titanium (Ti) bolometers. Devices with microbridge sizes ranging from 1 μm tttttttt 1 μm to 32 μm × 32 μm were fabricated and characterized. Resistance-temperature (R-T) measurements show that the critical temperature of the bolometers increases as the microbridge length decreases below 2 μm, corresponding to a length up to approximately 20 times the coherence length and 870 times the electron mean free path. This behavior is attributed to the longitudinal proximity effect. Analysis of the current-voltage (I-V) characteristics measured at different bath temperatures shows that the thermal conductance does not scale linearly with the microbridge area. This nonlinearity is likely caused by incomplete suppression of electron diffusion by the superconducting niobium (Nb) contacts as the Ti film thickness exceeds the proximity length within the Ti layer. This result suggests that optimizing the Ti film thickness could suppress electron diffusion and improve the overall performance of superconducting Ti bolometers.

title: xKAN: Explainable AI for Smartphone Based Indoor Localization using Wi-Fi Fingerprinting
authors: Akhil Singampalli, Gandraspalli Raja Reddy Sri Lakshmi, Sudeep Pasricha
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Machine learning (ML) models have become central to Wi-Fi based indoor localization, where noisy and highly variable fingerprints must be mapped to physical locations. Despite their strong performance, state-of-the-art ML models typically operate as opaque black boxes, offering limited visibility into the factors driving their predictions. This lack of intrinsic interpretability becomes even more critical in real indoor environments, where Wi-Fi data is inherently non-independent and non-identically distributed (non-IID) due to device heterogeneity, environmental dynamics, and floorplan-specific variations. Under these conditions, trust, reliability, and diagnosability remain major barriers to deploying ML-based indoor localization systems in practice. To address these challenges, we present xKAN, a post-hoc, model-agnostic explainability framework designed to improve the transparency of ML models for indoor localization. xKAN provides two complementary forms of explainability: global explainability, which summarizes how a model leverages Wi-Fi signals across an entire floorplan, and local explainability, which highlights the specific signal factors influencing an individual fingerprint’s prediction. Together, these perspectives reveal how broader Wi-Fi signal patterns shape local decision outcomes, uncovering hidden biases, inconsistencies, and region-specific behaviors within the ML model’s decision process. Experimental results demonstrate that xKAN offers a structured and intuitive pathway toward explainable AI driven indoor localization, with most relevant first (MoRF) evaluations showing that xKAN achieves up to 1.10× - 1.43× over SHAP and 1.03× - 1.38× over LIME in terms of faithfulness compared to state-of-the-art explainability methods. By improving transparency without modifying the underlying model, xKAN supports the development of more robust, reliable, and deployable localization systems for real-world environments.

title: Edge-End Heterogeneous Collaborative Learning by Prototype Selection and Edge Association
authors: Enze Yu, Haipeng Dai, Haihan Zhang, Yuben Qu, Tao Wu, Penghuan Cheng, Sujin Hou, Zhenzhe Zheng, Fan Wu, Guihai Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Edge-end collaborative learning trains models with exchanged knowledge through distributed interaction, alleviating the cloud's burden. Edge-end heterogeneous collaborative learning further enables edge servers and end devices to train models of different scales in parallel based on their computational capabilities. This technology supports various applications in different resource conditions and improves server resource utilization. However, implementing it is challenging due to heavy communication costs and high global costs (time and energy). To this end, this paper proposes a novel prototype-based edge-end heterogeneous collaborative learning method and an optimization algorithm, which improves model performance and reduces training costs. We first use prototypes to perform collaborative learning and analyze the convergence. Prototypes are computed as mean feature vectors from different classes. The aggregated prototypes help capture class information on end devices and generate data on edge servers. Then, we study how to determine prototype selection and edge association to minimize training time, energy consumption, and prototype approximation error under a limited reward budget, which is proven to be NP-hard. We split the original problem into two subproblems. The first is solved in the closed form. Through approximation and reformulation, the second is transformed into a submodular maximization problem with knapsack and matroid constraints. On this basis, we propose an approximation algorithm with a theoretical guarantee. Finally, by simulation and field experiments, our method takes 3.61% of communication costs to improve heterogeneous edge and end models' accuracy by at least 5.16% and 2.77% compared with five baselines. The proposed algorithm outperforms others by at least 9.78% in terms of global cost.

title: Intra-Class Amplitude Mixup for Data Augmentation in Image Classification
authors: Qiang Ren, Junli Wang, Changjun Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Mixup-based data augmentation improves deep neural network performance by enhancing training diversity, while amplitude mixing further enriches image style variations, benefiting tasks such as domain adaptation. However, the random selection of images for mixing in current amplitude-mixing approaches may introduce class-irrelevant information, potentially reducing their effectiveness in image classification. To address this, we propose Intra-Class Amplitude Mixup (ICAMix), a plug-and-play data augmentation method. To prevent random amplitude information from disrupting discriminative features in the mixed images, we restrict the mixing process to intra-class images, thereby enhancing the class discriminability of the generated images, making them more effective for classification tasks. Furthermore, since amplitude mixing preserves the structural integrity of images, our method effectively integrates multiple intra-class images, further enriching the diversity of features in the mixed images. Extensive experiments across various image classification tasks demonstrate that our method outperforms several baselines with minimal computational overheads. Visualizations of the generated images further validate its effectiveness. Our code is available at: https://github.com/rqfzpy/ICAMix.

title: CADKR: A Context-Aware Dialog-Based Knowledge Recommendation Model for Industrial Software Systems
authors: Tian-Yu Zuo, Pan Li, Xianghui Hu, Yichuan Jiang, Kai Di
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract:
Industrial software systems underpin complex industrial platforms by integrating cross-disciplinary expertize and sophisticated workflows. Their inherent complexity generates substantial cognitive demands, necessitating contextual, adaptive, and personalized knowledge support aligned with dynamic tasks and evolving expertize. Conventional recommendation approaches struggle to address heterogeneous knowledge sources, dynamic user needs with intent drift, and the domain-specific semantics required in industrial software. To overcome these challenges, we propose a context-aware dialog-based knowledge recommendation (CADKR) model, powered by large language models (LLMs). CADKR fuses dynamic interaction context with domain knowledge through a novel recommendation network (RecNet), enabling robust generalization to unseen scenarios and adaptability to preference drift. For practical deployment, lightweight optimization strategies compress model size by up to 98% without compromising accuracy. A case study in a large chemical industrial park demonstrates the effectiveness of CADKR in enhancing industrial knowledge support, while application deployments in leading new energy vehicle enterprises and the world’s largest circular resource power plant have yielded an independent verification report, providing strong evidence of the proposed method’s practical value.1

The report is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/verification-report. Code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CADKR-TCSS. To meet double-blind requirements, organization and personnel details are anonymized and will be disclosed after acceptance.

title: Adaptive QoS-Aware Service Composition in the Internet of Things Using a Hybrid Bayesian Network–Based Optimization Algorithm
authors: Seyed Salar Sefati, Seyedeh Tina Sefati, Alexandru Vulpe, Octavian Fratu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: As smart cities nowadays use many Internet of Things (IoT) devices, the need for service composition methods that are both efficient and flexible has become unavoidable. Methodologies should be designed to provide flexibility and sustain high quality of service (QoS) under dynamic urban conditions. Deterministic techniques often have poor scalability and require complete, accurate QoS information. In contrast, non-deterministic approaches can handle uncertainty but tend to be computationally expensive and less reliable under dynamic conditions. Service composition is considered an NP-hard problem due to the exponential complexity involved in selecting and combining optimal services under multiple QoS constraints. This paper proposes a novel hybrid framework called Bayesian Network–Grey Wolf Optimizer (BN-GWO), which integrates Bayesian Networks (BN) with the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) algorithm. The BN captures conditional dependencies among QoS attributes and accurately estimates missing values, while the GWO algorithm efficiently explores the compositional space to optimize QoS metrics. Experimental evaluations conducted using QoS traces synthetically generated via the iFogSim2 simulation platform demonstrate that the proposed BN-GWO framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple performance metrics.

title: Dynamic Power Allocation in NOMA-Based Satellite-Terrestrial Networks: A Customized Particle Swarm Optimization Approach
authors: Shuyuan Lu, Guanjun Xu, Lina Zhu, Yu Zhang, Youran Dong, Qinyu Zhang, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-based satellite-terrestrial systems can offer significant improvements in spectral efficiency. However, improper power allocation within these systems can result in resource waste, user unfairness, and performance degradation. This paper presents an innovative power allocation strategy for NOMA-based satellite-terrestrial communication systems, achieved through the development of an advanced optimization algorithm. Specifically, based on a representative multi-user system model that incorporates various real-world factors, such as polarization conversion loss, hardware imperfections, channel estimation errors, and imperfect successive interference, we derive the expressions for the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and outage probability. Then, we propose a customized particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm, termed DISH-PSO algorithm, that integrates Dirichlet-based initialization, decoding-priority-aware sorting, heuristic constraint handling, and exponentially decaying inertia weight into a unified framework. We design this customized PSO algorithm to optimize dynamic power allocation for minimizing outage probability. We demonstrate its enhanced convergence and robustness under complex communication conditions. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DISH-PSO algorithm consistently achieves a lower system outage probability and faster convergence than representative population-based, structured non-convex, and alternating optimization baselines, enabling the system to reach high-quality solutions with fewer iterations.

title: Data-Driven Robust Subspace Predictive Control With Embedded Disturbance Observer Structure
authors: Taejune Kong, Rogier Dinkla, Jan-Willem van Wingerden, Tom Oomen, Sehoon Oh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Subspace predictive control (SPC) is a data-driven control strategy that utilizes input–output measurements to predict future system behavior without requiring explicit model identification. Conventional SPC exhibits vulnerability to an unknown input disturbance, leading to degraded control performance and steady-state errors. To address these limitations, this article proposes a robust SPC method that inherently mitigates the effect of a constant input disturbance by augmenting the state-space representation through the internal model principle (IMP). This augmentation enables the controller to achieve integral action without requiring a separate disturbance observer (DOB) design. The proposed method is implemented in a data-driven framework, where an auxiliary disturbance is introduced into the data-driven algorithm to enhance disturbance rejection. A transfer function analysis verifies that the proposed Robust SPC eliminates a constant disturbance while maintaining the role of a DOB. Experimental validation on a two-inertia system confirms that the proposed method significantly improves reference tracking performance compared to conventional SPC, demonstrating its effectiveness in disturbance rejection without additional modeling complexity.

title: On Solving the Differential Direct Kinematics of Planar, Spherical, Orientational, and Translational Linkages
authors: Joseph Massin, Lionel Birglen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Robotics
abstract: Twists and wrenches, as defined in screw theory, are commonly used tools to establish the input/output velocity relationship of linkages, where the output velocity is written as the product of a Jacobian matrix and the input velocity array. However, for parallel mechanisms and, more generally, mechanisms with arbitrary topologies, this Jacobian matrix is typically derived through at least one numerical matrix inversion. This step may introduce numerical errors, particularly due to inconsistent units across the matrix components. This paper presents a new method for explicitly formulating the Jacobian matrix of a broad class of linkages. This class includes all planar and spherical linkages, but also parallel mechanisms whose end-effector may only translate or rotate about a fixed point, regardless of topological complexity. Central to this method is a new matrix called the twist matrix, constructed from appropriately chosen wrenches. Using this matrix, the Jacobian of any body within the mechanism, not just the moving platform, can be derived explicitly as a combination of elementary twist matrices, independently from the mechanism's complexity.

title: Performance Evaluation of Text Summarization Models for Content Extraction from PDFs
authors: Naveen Rathee, Rajeev Kumar Chauhan, Jaishanker Prasad Keshari, Jain Singh, Naveen Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Innovation for Sustainability (EmergIN)
abstract: Modern text summarizing systems rely heavily on Natural Language Processing (NLP), which makes it possible to automatically condense massive quantities of text into brief summaries. The influence of Natural Language Processing based summarization models (BERT, XLNet, and GPT-2) on large-scale text data processing is examined in this study. Utilizing methods like tokenization and part-of-speech tagging, the Text Summarizing Tool, powered by NLP, effectively condenses long documents. Analysis of biological and historical PDFs shows that GPT-2 consistently outperforms BERT and XLNet across a wide range of content types, as indicated by evaluation criteria like ROUGE. Line and group bar graphs, among other visual aids, offer a thorough overview of the models’ performance. In order to reduce information overload, the study’s conclusion highlights the revolutionary effects of NLP and summary models. It cuts through linguistic and cultural barriers, increasing information absorption, giving decision-makers better knowledge and promoting learning. With its ability to streamline the processing of large amounts of textual data and usher in a new era of effective information management, NLP has emerged as a crucial asset.

title: Fusion of Field Geometry Analysis and YOLO for Weed Detection in System of Rice Intensification Paddy Fields
authors: Priyanka Jayapal, Gayathri Ananthakrishnan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Cyber-Physical Systems (I5CPS)
abstract: Weed management presents a significant challenge in the System of Rice Intensification (SRI) paddy cultivation in Tamil Nadu, where the wide spacing planting and intermittent irrigation cause the growth of various weed types. Traditional weed control methods such as manual weeding (hand weeding), mechanical weeding (weeder tools) and chemical approaches (herbicides) require more labor, high cost and inconsistent in early growth stages when weeds resemble young paddy plants. This study proposes the fusion of the Radon transform (Line Detection integral transform) and YOLO (You Only Look Once) for better weed detection. This fusion helps to combine geometric row data with Deep Learning (DL) for highly accurate weed identification in System of Rice Intensification (SRI) fields. Images Captured by Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) in SRI planted fields were pre-processed using Vegetation enhancement (ExG index), Gaussian filtering, Otsu binary masking and morphological refinement. The preprocessed images were normalized and standardized for row alignment for weed detection. After preprocessing, the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Radon Transform achieved SRI plant row line with less than ±1.5° of angle error and less than 3 cm of paddy row space error. YOLOv12 was trained on 2070 labelled images to identify paddy crops and weeds by achieving 93.4% accuracy before Radon transform fusion. After fusion, each detection was checked against the nearest row centerline. This reduced the detection strength of weeds appearing on crop rows and reassigned crop detections that were far from the rows as weeds. The method reduced false positives by 42% improved weed detection between rows by 28% and increased the performance to 96.1% accuracy. The Radon transform with YOLOv12 generated row wise weed distribution hotspot locations and improved weed detection accuracy over deep learning methods.

title: Design of Anti-Collision Detection and Early Warning System for Highway Navigable Bridges Based on Object of Interest Imaging
authors: Hongxing Li, Changchun Yue, Zhihua Zhu, Jun Liu, Jun Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Smart City and Information System (ICSCIS)
abstract: With the development of social economy, the safe operation of navigable bridges has received more and more attention. Among them, the bridge anti-collision monitoring and early warning system has been widely used as a technical means to ensure the safe operation of navigable bridges. However, the commonly used bridge anti-collision early warning system has problems such as complex early warning technology path, high construction and maintenance cost, poor early warning accuracy, false alarm and insufficient real-time performance. Based on this, this paper proposes a new system scheme idea, which introduces hierarchical governance and object of interest imaging technology at the management and technical levels, and proposes a liberation scheme. It effectively solves the problems existing in the current bridge anti-collision warning system, and greatly guarantees the safe operation of navigable bridges.

title: Design and Analysis of a Terahertz DRA for Nextgeneration IoT and Smart Energy Systems
authors: Satya Prakash Singh, Prabhat Chandra Shrivastava, Prashant Kumar, Pradeep Kumar Tiwari, Manish Tiwari
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Power Electronics &amp; IoT Applications in Renewable Energy and its Control (PARC)
abstract: The given paper offers a design and analysis of a terahertz dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) that will be used in next-generation IoT and smart energy communication systems at terahertz frequencies. The proposed antenna is optimized to work at 2 THz resonance and the antenna has shown good impedance matching with S11 of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-\mathbf{3 6} \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex> and VSWR of nearly unity <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(=1.02)$</tex>. The radiation properties depict that there is a directivity of approximately 8.6 dBi and total radiation efficiency of approximately 95 % which implies a highpower transfer and minimal dielectric losses. The radiated power response increases linearly with frequency, reaching 0.006 W at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \text{THz}, 0.033 ~\mathrm{W}$</tex> at the resonance frequency of 2 THz, and 0.071 W at its highest frequency of operation of 3 THz. This makes the proposed antenna a candidate in the future in terms of high-speed, short-range terahertz links using the proposed antenna because of high efficiency and strong impedance matching and compact dielectric resonator configuration.

title: An IoT-Based Urban Monitoring System for Real-Time Urban Parameter Monitoring and Predictive Analytics
authors: Krati Rastogi Gupta, Nishtha Sharma, Sumit Kumar Sharma, Anurag Barthwal, Shwetank Avikal, Surbhi Vijh
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Power Electronics &amp; IoT Applications in Renewable Energy and its Control (PARC)
abstract: In the houses today, maintaining a favorable living condition is becoming more challenging, as the environmental factors are also changing unpredictably, the energy efficiency factors become more important, and occupants also require comfort. Basic thermostats and manual checks used as traditional home monitoring systems can only monitor little data but do not include the analytical capability. By them, the homeowner typically will not be able to have a perfect indoor environment that would result in a decreased level of comfort and energy efficiency. In this aspect, this study presents smart home on IoT with advanced features to determine the important environmental variables in real time. This involves data processing that is adaptive and air-quality threshold notifications to enable proactive home management, so as to ensure comfort and energy conservation. The system will assist the homeowner in getting valuable insights by constantly monitoring and analysing the internal environmental data, hence giving better insights in the decisions made, resource usage, reduction in the cost of energy and general quality of living.

title: CloudSim Simulation of Distributed Cloud Architecture: Performance, Cost, and Compliance Benefits
authors: Penumatcha Aparna Krishna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Cyber-Physical Systems (I5CPS)
abstract: Global enterprises that have their centralized web systems, and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP), deployed in the cloud encounter critical architectural choices that influence its performance, the cost of operation and compliance with regulations. These centralized architectures often introduce high network latency to international users and could contravene the laws of data sovereignty, like the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). In this paper, the author will analyze the trade-offs between the centralized, hybrid, and fully distributed cloud architecture of a global enterprise system. This paper has simulated three models using CloudSim 7.0: A centralized Web-based data center (DC) in the United States (US), a hybrid model comprising of regionally distributed web servers and a centralized US-based ERP, and a fully distributed model with co-located regionally web and ERP servers. Having made 30 simulation runs of each scenario, we discovered that, regardless of the fact that all the architectures also met the requirement of at least 6,500 ms service level agreement (SLA) baseline, the fully distributed architecture performed better than the rest in terms of performance and efficiency. The distributed model cut latency 99th percentile (P99) by half and the overall operational expenses by a quarter of what the centralized model carried out. It additionally removed any data transfer across regions, which was to prepare 100 percent compliance towards data localization requirements. These observations can be actually tested to show that a distributed architecture is the most reasonable solution to many global applications sensitive to latency which are characterized with maximum cost savings and regulatory compliance in any multiregional deployment.

title: University Students’ Use of Artificial Intelligence Applications in Learning: Patterns, Preferences, and Educational Implications
authors: Omran Ahmad Musleh, Mohammad Husien Faqeeh, Moath Kalef Al Omery, Abdelghani Remache, Salem Khalil Al Aqtash
publish: 2025 Global Congress on Emerging Technologies (GCET)
abstract: The present study aimed to explore the uses of artificial intelligence (AI) applications in the learning process among university students as well as to determine the relationship between AI application usage and several demographic variables. The study population consisted of all students registered in the University Requirement Arabic language courses as well as those registered in the Arabic Language Program at Al Ain University, which totals 241 students. The study sample included 209 students. To achieve the study objectives, the researchers developed a 24-item questionnaire, whose validity and reliability were verified through appropriate statistical procedures. The results indicated that the mean level of AI application use in the learning process among university students was high across all items, except for two that scored at a moderate level. Moreover, no statistically significant differences $(\alpha=0.05)$ were observed in the use of AI applications based upon gender, specialization, academic year, or cumulative GPA. Findings also revealed that ChatGPT was the most commonly used AI application in students’ learning processes, reflecting its widespread adoption as a tool for completing assignments, searching for information, and improving the quality of academic writing and reports. Other AI applications reported in the study instrument were rated at a moderate level.

title: Advanced VAPT Strategies Leveraging OSINT, Log Analytics and Machine Learning for Zero-Day Vulnerability Identification
authors: Manasa U, Arun Kumar B R, Neha P N
publish: 2025 International Conference On Emerging Computation and Information Technologies (ICECIT)
abstract: Traditional Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) tools primarily focus on known vulnerabilities, limiting their effectiveness against unknown or zero- day attacks. This paper proposes a unified framework called ML-VAPT Secure Scan that integrates Spider Foot-based OSINT reconnaissance, Splunk for SIEM log analytics, and Nessus for vulnerability scanning, enhanced with machine learning anomaly detection models such as Isolation Forest and LSTM. The system combines CVSS v3.1 and NIST SP 800-115 standards with an anomaly-based scoring model for proactive risk prioritization. Experimental results on a simulated enterprise environment demonstrate a detection accuracy improvement of 19%, reduced detection latency, and successful identification of zero-day behavioral anomalies. The proposed approach provides a scalable, automated, and intelligent VAPT pipeline suited for modern enterprise networks.

title: Improving Postgraduate Student's UX in Online Learning Platform for ODL Students at Taylor's University
authors: Muhammad Haziq Firdaus Mhd Arifin, Enna Ayub, Bob Cupitt
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Modern Educational Technology (ICMET)
abstract: This paper delves into iterative design enhancements in the Canvas Learning Management System (LMS), specifically focusing on the steps in the Iterative Design Phase of the Successive Approximation Model 2 (SAM2). The guiding frameworks for the design enhancement include the Jakob Nielsen's ten usability heuristics and the Web Content Accessibility Guideline (WCAG). This redesign includes simplifying instructional text, reorganising content, adding relevant visuals, removing distractions, and utilising interactive elements available in the platform. This nascent stage of the design-case based on Taylor's University, aims to create an intuitive and engaging online learning environment for its online distance learning (ODL) programme, and expand the field of knowledge in UX-informed instructional design.

title: Privacy Preserving ECG Anomaly Detection Using Differentially Private Generative Adversarial Network and Federated Learning
authors: S Rajesh, Harreeshnarain S, Jannath Nisha O S, K Uma Maheswari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Cyber-Physical Systems (I5CPS)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) anomaly detection is a major task that enables early detection of cardiovascular diseases. However, the sensitive nature of medical data poses significant challenges for privacy and data sharing across institutions. This paper introduces a novel model for electrocardiogram anomaly detection that incorporates an autoencoder and a differential privacy technique within a fully fledged federated learning approach. Unlike existing models, a high level of security can be ensured while achieving excellent anomaly detection results. The autoencoder model is trained in a decentralized manner across multiple healthcare institutions, allowing collaborative learning without sharing raw data. The integration of our custom differential privacy mechanism further enhances data security by adding controlled noise to the model updates, preventing potential privacy breaches. This innovation not only addresses the limitations of traditional centralized approaches but also outperforms conventional federated learning systems in terms of privacy guarantees. The experimental results demonstrate the proposed model’s high accuracy in detecting ECG abnormalities while respecting strict regulations on patient privacy.
